summary: Validated observed implementation only. This record has a concrete Win25H2Clean baseline for the raw CPU idle-state bundle, a machine-checkable apply and restore pass for the app's current profile, a current-build Ghidra no-match follow-up on ntoskrnl.exe, a fresh v3.1 runtime rerun on baseline-20260327-regprobe-visible-shell-stable that still fails during the set-candidate phase before reboot, a corrected benchmark rerun on the same baseline that still fails before any CPU or memory workload starts because Explorer does not come back in time during the snapshot-restore boot path, an earlier write-diagnostics lane that still could not emit a guest-side result file or wrapper error, and a later minimal VMware smoke plus direct registry write diagnostic that both succeeded. That narrows the remaining blocker toward the heavier ValidationController or WPR or reboot orchestration, not toward a broad guest execution or registry-write failure.

setting.name: CPU Idle States Bundle
setting.casual_explanation: CPU idle states let Windows save power when the processor has little to do. This tweak tries to reduce or bypass that behavior for latency-focused systems.
setting.why_people_change_it: People change it when chasing minimum latency for gaming, audio work, or other timing-sensitive workloads.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Reducing idle-state behavior can increase responsiveness in some narrow cases, but it can also raise heat and power consumption. The current app still uses raw power-manager registry values instead of a supported Microsoft control surface. On the fresh visible-shell baseline, the v3.1 runtime lane still fails during set-candidate before reboot and the corrected benchmark lane still fails before workload start because Explorer does not come back in time during the snapshot-restore boot path. Follow-up minimal diagnostics then showed that the general VMware guest execution chain and the direct raw bundle writes themselves both succeed, so the remaining blocker is narrower than a broad tooling failure.
setting.professional_notes: The app writes DisableIdleStatesAtBoot, IdleStateTimeout, and ExitLatencyCheckEnabled under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power. The local Power.txt notes also track these values with observed internal defaults of DisableIdleStatesAtBoot=0, ExitLatencyCheckEnabled=0, and IdleStateTimeout=500. The Win25H2Clean VM probe then showed the live baseline on this build as all three values missing, and confirmed a reversible apply of DisableIdleStatesAtBoot=1, IdleStateTimeout=0, and ExitLatencyCheckEnabled=1 before restoring back to missing. A current-build Ghidra follow-up then probed ntoskrnl.exe twice, once with the registry value names and once with the internal pseudocode names from the repo notes, and both passes came back with no string or xref matches. The original rebooted benchmark attempts from older shell-stable snapshots failed before any CPU or memory workload ran because Explorer did not return after reboot and the VM had to be recovered from snapshot. The later v3.1 runtime lane on the fresh baseline improved the evidence shape again by capturing baseline plus ETW start under the canonical evidence root, but it still failed during the set-candidate phase before reboot. After the wrapper parameter bug was fixed and automatic recovery was added, the corrected benchmark rerun on the same visible-shell baseline still failed before any workload started because Explorer did not come back in time during the snapshot-restore boot path. A later dedicated write-diagnostics runner then tried to isolate the write stage itself with provider and reg.exe methods, both with and without WPR, under two different guest output roots. Those diagnostics still did not emit a guest-side result file or wrapper error, even though shell recovery stayed clean after snapshot restore. Follow-up minimal VMware tooling smoke then proved that the generic guest execution and host copy-back chain was healthy under C:\RegProbe-Diag, and a direct minimal registry-write diagnostic showed both provider and reg.exe writes succeeding and restoring the bundle back to missing under the same guest root. That narrows the remaining ambiguity away from broad guest execution or registry write rights and toward the heavier ValidationController, WPR, or reboot orchestration used by the failing runtime and benchmark lanes. This is enough to keep the bundle well-scoped and evidence-backed, but not enough to treat it as an app-ready supported control surface.

allowed_values.label: Observed Win25H2Clean baseline
allowed_values.meaning: The current 25H2 VM baseline leaves DisableIdleStatesAtBoot, IdleStateTimeout, and ExitLatencyCheckEnabled unset.

allowed_values.label: Observed app latency-focused bundle
allowed_values.meaning: The current app writes the raw latency-focused bundle that the VM probe applied and restored cleanly.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes a raw CPU idle-state registry bundle, but this review did not find a primary Microsoft registry surface that validates it as a supported tuning method.

windows_defaults.label: Observed Win25H2Clean baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Current 25H2 builds where the raw CPU idle-state bundle is left unset

states.rationale: Leave the raw CPU idle-state bundle unset and let Windows manage processor idle behavior.

windows_defaults.notes: This is the current observed VM baseline, not a primary Microsoft registry contract.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems using standard Windows processor power management
avoid_for: None until a supported Microsoft mapping is confirmed

states.rationale: Keep the raw CPU idle-state bundle unset and stay on the current observed baseline.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want supported Windows processor power management behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You are trying to force undocumented raw registry tuning.
tradeoffs: Windows keeps its current Windows-managed idle-state behavior.
The app's raw bundle stays documented but research-gated.

recommended_profiles.label: Current app profile

states.rationale: Use the current app bundle that the VM probe applied and restored cleanly.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You are comparing the app's raw idle-state bundle against the current observed baseline.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want only supported Microsoft power controls.
tradeoffs: Can reduce idle-state behavior in latency-focused experiments.
Can also raise heat and power use, and repeated rebooted benchmark lanes on Win25H2Clean dropped shell availability before any workload ran.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Introduction to Processor Idle States
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents processor idle states as a Windows power-management feature area, but this source does not publish the raw registry bundle used by the app.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes DisableIdleStatesAtBoot, IdleStateTimeout, and ExitLatencyCheckEnabled under the Power key.

evidence.title: nohuto power trace for DisableIdleStatesAtBoot
evidence.summary: Power.txt lists DisableIdleStatesAtBoot in the power values block, matching the first element of the app's raw idle-states bundle.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean reversible probe for the CPU idle-state bundle
evidence.summary: The current 25H2 VM baseline had all three values missing, the app bundle wrote DisableIdleStatesAtBoot=1, IdleStateTimeout=0, and ExitLatencyCheckEnabled=1, and the probe restored all three back to missing.

evidence.title: Repo power notes
evidence.summary: The repo tracks these power-manager values as research notes, but this review did not find a primary Microsoft registry mapping for publishing them as a validated preset.

evidence.title: Our Ghidra follow-up - ntoskrnl CPU idle string/xref probes
evidence.summary: Two current-build ntoskrnl.exe probes looked for the registry names and for the internal pseudocode names tracked in the repo notes. Both came back with no matching strings or xrefs, so this pass did not produce a current-build kernel decompilation lead for the bundle.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean rebooted CPU idle benchmark incident
evidence.summary: Repeated rebooted benchmark attempts from older shell-stable snapshots did not reach the CPU or memory workload phases because Explorer did not return after reboot. After the wrapper parameter bug was fixed and automatic recovery was added, a benchmark rerun on baseline-20260327-regprobe-visible-shell-stable still failed before any CPU or memory workload artifacts were produced because Explorer did not come back in time during the snapshot-restore boot path.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean v3.1 runtime lane for the CPU idle-state bundle
evidence.summary: The original v3.1 runtime lane captured the baseline state with all three values missing and started a WPR GeneralProfile trace, but the guest program returned non-zero before the candidate or post-boot stage completed. A rerun on baseline-20260327-regprobe-visible-shell-stable reproduced the failure more precisely: baseline capture and WPR start succeeded, but the lane still failed during the set-candidate stage before reboot and then required snapshot recovery.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean write diagnostics for the CPU idle-state bundle
evidence.summary: A dedicated write-diagnostics lane tried to isolate provider and reg.exe writes under two guest output roots. All four attempts failed before any guest-side result file or wrapper error was produced, while shell recovery after snapshot restore stayed clean. That made the remaining blocker look more like guest execution or tooling semantics than a missing ordinary rerun.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean minimal VMware guest tooling smoke
evidence.summary: A minimal vmrun guest execution and copy-back smoke under C:\RegProbe-Diag wrote a text file, emitted environment and result JSON, and copied all three artifacts back to the repo evidence root. That disproves a broad failure of the VMware guest execution or host copy chain on this baseline.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean minimal direct registry write diagnostic for the CPU idle-state bundle
evidence.summary: A direct guest-side diagnostic under C:\RegProbe-Diag wrote DisableIdleStatesAtBoot=1, IdleStateTimeout=0, and ExitLatencyCheckEnabled=1 with both the PowerShell provider and reg.exe, then restored the bundle to missing and copied the result back successfully. That narrows the remaining CPU idle blocker away from simple registry write rights and toward the heavier WPR or reboot orchestration.

decision.why: The repo power notes and the Win25H2Clean probe line up on the same raw bundle and show both the current observed baseline and the app profile. The current-build Ghidra follow-up still did not surface a direct ntoskrnl lead. On the fresh visible-shell baseline, the v3.1 runtime lane still failed during the set-candidate phase before reboot and the corrected benchmark lane still failed before any workload started because Explorer did not come back in time during the snapshot-restore boot path. An earlier dedicated write-diagnostics lane still produced no guest-side result file, but follow-up minimal VMware smoke and direct registry-write diagnostics both succeeded under C:\RegProbe-Diag. That proves the generic guest execution chain and the raw bundle writes themselves are healthy on this baseline. The remaining blocker is therefore the heavier ValidationController, WPR, or reboot orchestration, and the record still is not an app-ready supported control surface.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: before: all three values null; after: DisableIdleStatesAtBoot=1, IdleStateTimeout=0, ExitLatencyCheckEnabled=1; restored: all three values null
validation_proof.notes: Guest VM probe captured the exact before, after, and restored registry bundle and returned the machine to baseline. The repo power notes also track these values with internal defaults of 0, 500, and 0 when the raw bundle is present. Later rebooted benchmark lanes failed before any CPU or memory workload started because Explorer did not return after reboot, and the VM had to be recovered from snapshot. A fresh v3.1 runtime rerun on baseline-20260327-regprobe-visible-shell-stable then captured baseline plus ETW start under the canonical evidence root, but still failed during the set-candidate stage before reboot and again required snapshot recovery. After the wrapper parameter bug was fixed and automatic recovery was added, the corrected benchmark lane on the same baseline still failed before any workload started because Explorer did not come back in time during the snapshot-restore boot path. A later dedicated write-diagnostics runner also failed to emit a guest-side result file or wrapper error under two guest output roots. Follow-up minimal VMware smoke then wrote and copied back a text file plus JSON artifacts successfully, and a direct minimal registry diagnostic showed both provider and reg.exe writes succeeding and restoring the bundle back to missing under C:\RegProbe-Diag. That proves the unresolved ambiguity is no longer a broad guest execution or registry write failure.
